hints: For two coprime values a,b, the largest non-representable amount is the Frobenius number ab−a−b.
solution: For coprime denominations $5$ and $21$, the largest value not representable is the Frobenius number $5\cdot21-5-21=105-26=79.$

hints: Comparing two variances uses the F statistic = ratio of sample variances.
solution: The test statistic for comparing two variances is $F=\dfrac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}=\dfrac{0.0003}{0.0001}=3.$

hints: Test H₀:p=1/3 vs p>1/3 with p̂=0.4, n=1000.
solution: Testing $p=\tfrac13$ against $p>\tfrac13$: $Z=\dfrac{0.4-\tfrac13}{\sqrt{\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23/1000}}\approx4.47$, well beyond any standard critical value, so we reject $H_0$ — customers do prefer plan $A$: answer $1.$

hints: Relative speeds: d/(v_A−v_B)=7 and (Alice is 3d behind Carter) 3d/(v_A−v_C)=12.
Then v_B−v_C=(v_A−v_C)−(v_A−v_B)=d/4−d/7=3d/28; Bob reaches Carter at 2d/(v_B−v_C)=56/3 min.
After Alice passes Carter (12 min): 56/3−12=20/3 min = 400 s.
solution: From relative speeds: $v_A-v_B=\tfrac d7$ and $v_A-v_C=\tfrac{3d}{12}=\tfrac d4$, so $v_B-v_C=\tfrac d4-\tfrac d7=\tfrac{3d}{28}$. Bob (initially $2d$ behind Carter) catches Carter at $\tfrac{2d}{3d/28}=\tfrac{56}{3}$ min after the instant, i.e. $\tfrac{56}{3}-12=\tfrac{20}{3}$ min after Alice passes Carter $=400$ seconds.

hints: Find the smallest coin count where 60¢ has two different (nickel,dime,quarter) breakdowns.
With 6 coins: (0 q, 6 d, 0 n) and (1 q, 2 d, 3 n) both total 60¢.
solution: We need the fewest coins for which $60$¢ is ambiguous. With $6$ coins, both $6$ dimes and $\{1\text{ quarter},2\text{ dimes},3\text{ nickels}\}$ total $60$¢, so the counts can't be determined. No smaller $N$ is ambiguous, so $N=6.$

hints: Total shaded = cs; if every row had ≤ t−1, then cs ≤ r(t−1).
So c > r(t−1)/s forces some row to reach t: c = ⌊r(t−1)/s⌋ + 1.
solution: If no row reaches $t$, every row has $\le t-1$ black squares, so $cs\le r(t-1)$. Thus $c=\big\lfloor\tfrac{r(t-1)}{s}\big\rfloor+1$ forces a row with $\ge t$. For $(30,21,14)$: $\lfloor\tfrac{600}{14}\rfloor+1=42+1=43.$

hints: Total value of all 8 bills = 200; each drawn bill averages 200/8=25.
solution: The eight bills total $\$200$, so each selected bill averages $\$25$. The expected sum of $7$ is $7\cdot25=\$175.$

hints: A path is an arrangement of 3 x-moves, 4 y-moves, 5 z-moves.
solution: Each path is an ordering of $3+4+5=12$ unit moves: $\dfrac{12!}{3!\,4!\,5!}=27720.$

hints: Position k wins iff the first k−1 birthdays are distinct AND yours matches one of them.
The optimum is near √365 ≈ 19–20; it peaks at position 20.
solution: You win at position $k$ if the first $k-1$ people all have distinct birthdays and yours matches one of them: $P(k)=\Big(\prod_{i=1}^{k-2}\tfrac{365-i}{365}\Big)\cdot\tfrac{k-1}{365}$. Maximizing this discrete product (the optimum sits near $\sqrt{365}\approx19$) gives the peak at position $20.$

hints: The ratio L=lim F_{n+1}/F_n satisfies L=1+1/L.
solution: Dividing $F_{n+1}=F_n+F_{n-1}$ by $F_n$ and taking the limit gives $L=1+\tfrac1L$, i.e. $L^2-L-1=0$, so $L=\tfrac{1+\sqrt5}{2}$. Thus $a=1,b=5,c=2$ and $abc=10.$
